title: World's First Data Center in Space: Genius or Madness

segments.text:
Starcloud one separation confirmed. This is 
the first launch of a data center in space.

And here Elon and Jensen seriously discussing 
putting them on orbit. My first thought was,

are you serious? Then it got far more interesting 
because it's not just them. Google, Amazon,

everyone is circling the same idea. There is 
something about space that keeps pulling us back.

the next step beyond Earth data centers is our our 
Earth orbital data centers. and we'll be launching

with SpaceX orbital data centers at the 100 to 200 
gigawatt per year. What if you want to go beyond

a mere terawatt per year? In order to do that, you 
have to go to the moon. I've spent the last decade

designing microchips that live or die by physics. 
That's why this episode is personal because this

idea sits right on the knife's edge because 
it's either pure madness or inevitable next

step. Subscribe to the channel and let me explain. 
We are building gigawatt data centers. And these

are not just big buildings with servers. It's AI 
supercomputers the size of cities, millions of

GPUs. And we built power plants next door just to 
keep them alive. And the most unglamorous part is

cooling. Because these campuses don't just consume 
electricity, they devour water. They drain rivers

just to stop silicon from cooking itself to death. 
But what's becoming even more scarce than power

and water is time. That's why hyperscalers are in 
a land grab. Speed is a mode now. But on Earth,

permits take years. Land is limited. Power grid 
is sold out. And the road map says we need 100

times more compute by 2030. If this trend 
continues, we don't just run out of power,

we run out of Earth. So maybe it's time to put the 
Cloud in the clouds. I know it sounds ridiculous,

but space is tempting. There is no land limits 
and in theory unlimited solar power. It sounds

clean and infinite, but this idea is insane 
on so many levels. That's why I can't wait to

unpack it today. We will do it in two layers. 
Plan A, put data centers in orbit. Plan B,

put data centers on the Moon. So, what would it 
actually take to build a tiny 20,000 GPU data

center and make it fly over your head? Before we 
launch anything, we have to be honest about what

an AI data center is. Because launching GPUs 
to space is easy. Launching a functioning AI

data center is not. On Earth, a data center is 
surrounded by invisible infrastructure. Power

comes from somewhere else. Cooling is an entire 
science. Fibers stitches thousands of servers into

one giant AI supercomputer. In orbit, all of that 
disappears. So, let's run the checklist. First, to

launch a data center into orbit, you need compute. 
That's the easy part. We already know how to build

absurdly powerful GPUs. But the moment you leave 
Earth, silicon enters a different battlefield.

Space is brutal to chips. It's constantly 
bombarding them with high energy particles. So,

we need chips that can survive this huge amount 
of radiation. You don't see these particles,

but they flip bits in memory, corrupt 
calculations, slowly degrade transistors.

Space slowly kills your servers, one bit flip 
at a time. On Earth, the atmosphere and magnetic

field absorb most of that. In low Earth orbit, you 
still get partial protection. So, it's survivable,

but not forgiving, which means you can't just 
throw a rack of GPUs into orbit. You have to

shield them from all the sides carefully. We 
need physical shielding that blocks particles

without adding too much mass. Technically, 
this is solvable and we already have proof.

Startup Starcloud already sent NVIDIA Hopper GPU 
into space and it's already about 100 times more

powerful than any computer previously operated 
in orbit and it's already running inference in

space. So the question is no longer can GPUs 
survive up there. The question is does it

make any sense economically and technically? 
Because the moment you turn these GPUs on,

they start pulling power. And power is the least 
insane part of that story. Because on Earth,

you have clouds, night, bad weather. In 
orbit, you just get sunlight and a lot of

it. At first glance, it looks perfect. 
You unfold solar arrays, face the sun,

collect energy, run your systems, charge batteries 
for eclipse. repeat every orbit. At small scale,

it works beautifully until it is a data center 
because the moment you scale to real AI compute,

everything changes. On Earth, modern AI campuses 
already sit in the gigawatt range. Of course,

the first orbital proposals are more modest, tens 
of megawatts. Starcloud, for example, is aiming

at roughly 40 megawatts. That's about 300 NVLink 
72 racks with about 20,000 GPUs in total. Now,

let's run the numbers. 10 square meters of solar 
panels gives you just a few kilowatts. Scale that

to 40 megawatts and the geometry explodes. You're 
looking at something like a 350x350 m solar array.

over 100,000 square meters and roughly 400 
tons. Just picture it. Dozens of football

fields of solar panels flying over your head at 
nearly 8 kilometers per second. That's massive,

but not impossible if delivered in pieces. And the 
upside is real. In orbit, solar outperforms ground

systems by at least 10 times. Imagine we shoot 
all of that to space and solve power. Finally,

we've got an AI data center that we won't be 
able to plug off anymore. And just as instantly,

every watt of power you generated turns into your 
new problem, heat. That's when the dream collides

with physics. Which sounds ridiculous, right? 
Because space is cold. And that single idea misled

more engineers than anything else because your 
GPU doesn't care about the temperature in space.

It cares whether the heat can escape silicon. 
Everyone thinks that space is cold. That's why

this problem is so dangerous. In low Earth orbit, 
you're essentially sitting in near perfect vacuum.

There is no air, no medium to carry heat away. 
And the higher you go, the more absolute the

vacuum becomes. Space is cold. But vacuum is 
an insulator which actually make things much

harder to cool in space. On Earth, heat disappears 
easily. Air molecules collide with hot surfaces

and carry energy off. Water pulls heat away even 
faster. In space, none of that exists. You cannot

dump heat into space. You can only radiate it 
away and radiation is slow. Typically, it's

hundreds of times less effective than convection 
or liquid cooling on Earth. That's why satellites

carry radiators, huge panels whose only job is to 
dump waste heat into darkness. This is where the

fantasy meets thermodynamics. Radiative cooling 
follows this Stefan-Boltzmann Law. In plain terms,

if you want to dump megawatts of heat without 
melting your hardware, you add surface area,

a lot of it. For a 40 megawatt orbital data 
center, the math lands in the range of 120,000

square meters effective area. That's about 15 to 
20 football fields of radiator wings in orbit.

This means radiators must be huge compared to the 
relative compute load and most likely implemented

as two-sided panels. But here is the critical 
part. Radiator mass compounds the problem. It

scales with how much heat you have to reject. 
If we are using a standard spacecraft estimates,

you're looking at roughly 400 to 800 tons of 
radiator hardware for a 40 megawatt data center.

And that's just for cooling. At the current launch 
prices, that's in the range of $2 to4 billion just

to launch the radiators. And when launch cost will 
drop to something $500 per kilogram with Starship,

you're looking at something like $200 million to 
$400 million just to launch the cooling. So yes,

Starship helps. But even in the best case, we are 
looking at hundreds of millions of dollars just

to launch cooling. And surprisingly, from here on, 
it's only getting worse and worse. Electronics in

orbit don't sit at one temperature. In low Earth 
orbit, a spacecraft circles the planet every 90

minutes. One moment, it's in direct sunlight 
at 120°. Minutes later, it drops into shadow

at minus 170. That's a 300° temperature swing. And 
this thermal stress accumulates over its lifespan.

every orbit all day, every day. So in space, you 
need a different approach. That's exactly what

Starlink does. The electronics inside is wrapped 
into multi-layer insulation. Thin reflective foils

that block most solar radiation and trap internal 
heat. Small onboard heaters kick in during eclipse

so batteries and processors don't freeze. The 
result is a controlled internal range from 10

to 60° inside while outside space is trying 
to tear it apart. It works at satellite scale

at few hundred W. But now scale it to a megawatt 
data center and that's a huge jump with no proven

solution. So suddenly the cold vacuum of space 
become the hottest engineering problem. on paper

Space data centers look elegant, but the image 
changes fast. It's not GPUs on the orbit,

it's radiators and panels in orbit. But even if 
you solve heat, you still need to talk to Earth.

Because if the results can't get down, the compute 
doesn't matter. It's just a very expensive space

heater. Next, we will discuss the real killer of 
data centers in space and wrap up the economics

and see if it makes sense to build a data center 
on the moon instead. But before we go there,

I want to talk to you about my favorite tech 
magazine - IEEE Spectrum. It keeps me sharp on

what's happening across the industry, including 
semiconductors, AI, robotics, energy, and space

tech. When I want real depth and high quality 
analysis, IEEE Spectrum is where I go. They

consistently cover the trends that actually matter 
across the tech stack. Running a tech company and

making content in tech means I need signal, not 
noise. And that's exactly what I get there. I

actually enjoy reading both digital and physical 
magazines. Digital gives you instant access,

archives, and newsletters. Great for staying 
current, but the print edition is special. You

sit down with a cup of coffee and really spend 
time with it without distractions. I love to

keep past issues on my desk and revisit them. 
That's my steadiest source of ideas. If you

care about staying ahead in tech and engineering, 
subscribe to IEEE Spectrum now through the link

below and you will get an exclusive 10% off 
your subscription. If you enjoy my content,

you will most certainly love their content, 
too. And to me, that means a lot that they are

sponsoring this episode. Now, back to the space 
dream. And now, things will get uncomfortable

because on Earth, data centers are stitched 
together with fiber. Inside modern AI clusters,

links are already pushing towards 1.6 terabits per 
second. That's the baseline these systems expect.

constant high throughput communication between 
racks, clusters and users. So the moment you put a

supercomputer on orbit, a question arises, how do 
you move that much data back to Earth? In theory,

you can send enormous amounts of data through 
space using lasers. This is called free space

orbital communication. You fire extremely precise 
laser beams between satellites and those beams

carry data at hundreds of gigabits per second. 
Starlink already use it between satellites inside

space Band can be actually incredible. We are 
talking about hundreds of gigabits per second

because there is no atmosphere, no cables, just 
light. The real bottleneck is getting that data

back to Earth. Because the moment this signal hits 
the atmosphere, everything changes. Clouds scatter

light. Weather interferes. Even turbulence in the 
air distorts the beam. You can work around some

of that with phase arrays. These are flat antennas 
that steer and focus the signal into a very tight

beam. So tight that enough of it reaches Earth. 
It helps, but it's still nowhere near fiber

level capacity. So, here you end up with a strange 
mismatch. Massive compute in orbit and very narrow

pipelines down to Earth. And the thing is, the 
further from Earth you go, the worse it gets. And

we will come back to that in a moment when talking 
about the Moon. By now, the pattern is clear.

Power is solvable. Cooling is brutal. bandwidth is 
the weak spot. And even if you solve all of that,

eventually something will break because GPUs 
fail. Components age and in about 4 years

they are obsolete anyway. So imagine you want to 
replace a failed unit or upgrade the cluster. How

do you go about maintenance? On Earth, we walk in, 
replace a rack, swap a part, and move on in orbit.

That's impossible. So the whole thing is 
designed for survival or for replacement. Both

hurt. The moment you put a data center in space, 
physical maintenance disappears. No technicians,

no quick fixes. Everything has to keep running 
on its own. So here the philosophy changes

completely. You don't fix things in orbit. You 
just outnumber the failure. And this forces you to

launch more capacity than you actually need. Extra 
nodes sitting idle, ready to take over the second

something glitches. If one server dies, the system 
reroots around it. The fleet slowly refreshing

itself over time. Here, software becomes the 
first line of maintenance. Most of the things are

fixed remotely and from the outside it looks like 
nothing happened. Only when hardware truly fails,

you accept the loss and then you wait for the 
next launch window and send up a replacement.

This is how Starlink already works. They don't 
repair units, they replace them. New units go up

constantly. Old or failed ones de-orbit and burn 
up in the atmosphere. Right now, it's happening

at a steady cadence of two satellites a day. 
Satellites coming down, new ones going up.

Most of the debris vaporizes before it reaches 
the ground. The problem here, it doesn't just

disappear. What's left becomes a thin layer of 
particles high in the atmosphere. Aluminum oxides

and other remnants that can linger for years. 
Scientists are still studying what that means

for ozone and climate. Well, at small satellite 
scale, this model works. At data center scale,

it's brutal. Moreover, every upgrade is tied to 
a rocket and suddenly economics start to dominate

the conversation because redundancy means extra 
hardware. Extra hardware means extra launches and

remember tons of solar and radiators and all of 
that becomes very expensive. And this is where the

romance ends and the spreadsheet begins because 
if it's not cheaper or strategically necessary,

it won't scale. Let's go back to our 40 megawatts 
orbital data center. That's about 400 tons of

compute plus 400 tons of radiators plus 400 
tons of solar. So more than 1,000 tons total

mass. At today's launch prices, about $5,000 per 
kilogram, you are looking at at least $5 billion

just to lift a system like this into orbit. And 
that's only to get the mass of the planet. It

doesn't include solar panel costs, compute cost, 
batteries, it doesn't include the cooling. And if

you add all of this infrastructure, the costs are 
just exploding. we coming to the kind of numbers

that make even the richest hyperscalers pause. In 
the end, it all comes down to one simple matrix,

watts per dollar. And at the moment, I'm not 
convinced the math here works. And I know at the

moment this idea might already look like madness 
to you. Well, at some point it might start making

sense because launch costs are falling so fast. 
Companies like SpaceX and Stoke are pushing that

curve down. Internal launch costs for SpaceX are 
likely far lower, at least five times, than public

pricing. That's the only reason why anyone serious 
is even talking about orbital data centers. And

it's also why Elon keeps bringing it up. But I 
feel like we are still missing a breakthrough

or two to get there. But at the same time, the 
orbit is getting really crowded. There are already

thousands of satellites up there. Put something 
the size of a floating data center into low orbit

and congestion becomes real. If you need to scale 
it to one terawatt, you need to go to the Moon.

Well, data centers on the Moon, that's a real 
moonshot. Why to build a data center on the Moon

at all? Because Earth has limits. power, land, 
permits, politics. The Moon has none of those.

Vast surface area. In theory, unlimited expansion. 
That's the attraction. But guess what? The further

you go from Earth, the harder everything gets. 
And when you try to put a data center on the Moon,

our checklists get brutal. Let's run it. First, 
compute. Radiation on the moon is far harsher than

in low Earth's orbit. There is no magnetic field, 
no atmosphere, nothing between your hardware and

space. Cosmic rays heat constantly. But solar 
storms is the real nightmare. A solar storm is

a massive cloud of charged particles that can 
last for hours, sometimes days. On the moon,

it hits directly. No protection. That means 
a steady bombardment of high energy particles

slamming into silicon, flipping bits, corrupting 
memory, triggering logic errors. A normal GPU

would not survive long, a couple of days, maybe 
a week. So we need a radiation hardened chips,

but that's explodes complexity and the chip size. 
Then we need to add heavy shielding around the

hardware and typically use a specialized chip 
process like silicon-on-insulator. Basically,

instead of building transistors directly on 
silicon, you isolate them on a thin insulating

layer. That separation reduces the chances that 
the particle strike triggers a cascade of current

through the chip. It makes circuits far more 
tolerant to radiation. And yes, all of that works,

but it's expensive. Radiation hardened chips 
can cost 10 to 100 times more than normal chips,

and they are usually slower. You don't want 
to put cutting edge 2 nanometer GPU on the

Moon. The newest nodes are the most fragile. 
Transistors are so small now that a single high

energy particle can do more than flip a bit. It 
can knock out an entire computing core. With every

transistor shrink with smaller and smaller nodes, 
performance goes up, resilience goes down. Also,

some suggest that you can just keep all this 
heavy protection. And of course, you could,

but then you bet that your multi-billion dollar 
lunar supercluster will survive more than a

month. Probably it won't. So you fall back 
to more mature nodes like 7 nanometer. AMD

has already some radiation hardened designs like 
SOC's and FPGAs but so far no radiation hardened

GPUs. These are reliable but not the cutting edge 
which means you already trading the performance

for survival. And as if it wasn't enough, the Moon 
adds one more complication dust. fine, abrasive

electrostatic dust that clings to everything. It 
coats radiators, degrades objects, interferes with

moving parts, slowly killing hardware over time. 
And that's before you've even turned the system

on. Because in the end, compute isn't the hardest 
problem. Power is. At first glance, this sounds

easy. Solar energy is abundant on the Moon. There 
is no atmosphere, no clouds, just pure sunlight.

Perfect, right? Not quite. The Moon rotates 
slowly. One Moon or day lasts about 29 Earth

days. That means roughly 14 days of continuous 
sunlight followed by 14 days of complete darkness.

During the darkness, solar output drops to zero. 
So now you need either massive battery storage or

nuclear reactors deployed on the lunar surface. 
Both add mass mass adds launch cost. And once

again launch cost dominates everything. Let's 
assume somehow you solved power and can survive

two week night. Now you finally have a lunar data 
center. And now comes the question that ruins the

mood. How do you cool it? Because on the Moon, 
heat has no place to go. No air, no wind, no

atmosphere to carry it away. But now at the Moon, 
extreme temperature swings. Surface temperatures

can swing from over 120° C in sunlight to 
below - 130° C at night. In shadowed craters,

it drops even lower. Your hardware isn't just 
hot. It's expanding and contracting constantly.

Just like in orbit, radiators become critical. 
They have to be enormous, disproportionately

large compared to the compute they're cooling. I'm 
sure at this point many of you are already ready

to give up on this crazy idea. But we haven't 
covered the worst part yet because the biggest

killer isn't the heat. It's physics. Specifically, 
the speed of light. The Moon sits nearly 400,000

kilometers away, which means every bit of data 
has to cross that distance. The only realistic

way to move large volumes here is through laser 
optical communication. High power infrared beams

aimed directly at optical ground stations on 
Earth. Interior, this works beautifully. Laser

links can reach gigabits level speed, which 
is enough for bulky data transfer. But light,

light is only so fast. It takes about 1.3 seconds 
to travel one way from the moon to Earth. Even

under perfect conditions, you're looking at 
roughly 2.6 seconds of round trip latency.

That severely limits the list of applications. So 
it won't work for real-time applications like AI

inference for what we use terrestrial data centers 
for, but it might work for data storage or large

jobs where you process everything up there and 
then send the results down afterwards. Compare it

with low Earth's orbit where latency sits around 
10 to 50 milliseconds. The Moon feels distant. So

what would make sense up there? probably storage. 
But then you can ask an obvious question. Isn't

storage already way too expensive on Earth? And 
yet this is exactly what Lonear data holding is

experimenting with offplanet data storage. This 
is a strategy to keep the most valuable important

data of Earth. So if something goes wrong here, 
this is a way to back us up. Lonestar planned to

build up tiny lunar data centers. They launched 
a test mission in March 2025, so last year,

with 1 kilogram of compute plus SSD storage. But 
unfortunately, the landing didn't succeed. Still,

the direction is clear. The long game isn't 
streaming or real-time compute. It's backup. If

something catastrophic happens on Earth, the data 
survives somewhere else, cold storage. And yes,

extremely expensive one. So, if you're not 
yet convinced that this whole idea is a little

lunatic, let's talk about the final killer, 
economics. Getting mass to orbit is expensive.

Landing payloads on the Moon can cost 20 times 
more per kilogram than low Earth orbit. Everything

must land softly, which is not guaranteed. So, 
a small lunar data center quickly becomes a mega

expensive project. Basically, you have to solve 
everything we've discussed for orbit and then

add even more maintenance robots, replacement 
hardware, and autonomous repair. It's extremely

hard to run a lunar data center entirely from 
Earth. At some point, you need local capability,

mining, possibly silicon because the Moon has 
plenty of it. And then manufacturing. A data

center won't be enough. You need economy. That's 
why for me the only possible reason to build a

data center on the Moon is to build the economy 
on the Moon. Otherwise, every problem that's

already hard and expensive to solve on Earth 
is becoming even harder and more expensive to

solve up there. So, our space data centers 
madness or inevitability. If we take orbit,

the physics says it's possible. Economics says not 
yet. The Moon at the moment feels a little bit out

of the orbit. Even with all the free land, 
all the sunlight, all the theoretical scale,

is any of this worth the effort? Still, there 
is something about space we can stay away from.

And I personally think we are still missing a 
couple of breakthroughs in energy and the way

we compute before we can make such an endeavor. 
Subscribe to the channel not to miss them. Now

watch this episode on the world's largest AI 
data center or this one where I take you inside

the secret chip factory to show you the smallest 
microchips to date. And I will see you there. Ciao
